title: Jacobaea echaeta
taxonomy: Jacobaea echaeta is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family (Asteraceae). It belongs to the genus Jacobaea, which is a group of thistle-like plants. The plant is native to the Mediterranean region and is often found in dry, rocky areas. From a taxonomic perspective, Jacobaea echaeta is classified as a member of the tribe Cardueae, within the family Asteraceae. Further classification would place it within the family of Compositae, now more commonly referred to as Asteraceae.

benefits: Jacobaea echaeta, also known as the knapweed or thistle, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine due to its potential health benefits. intro: Jacobaea echaeta, also known as Jacob's ladder, is a perennial plant species in the daisy family (Asteraceae) characterized by its tall, sparsely branched stems and clusters of small, yellow flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Jacobaea echaeta include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Jacobaea echaeta is commonly used to prepare teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to treat a range of ailments, although its use should be approached with caution due to potential toxicity.
